summary: Joe Reis defends the continued relevance of Kimball dimensional modeling against critics who claim columnar storage and unlimited compute have made it obsolete. He argues that dismissing data modeling in favor of raw performance leads to incomprehensible data warehouses that fail to deliver business value, ultimately resulting in users reverting to Excel and data teams getting cut.
key_points: Despite having unprecedented tools and compute power, the data industry still struggles to deliver business value
A lack of a data model is still a data model - just a bad one that creates confusion
Performance should be addressed last during physical data modeling, not prioritized from the start
Equating all data modeling with Kimball reveals how limited data modeling knowledge has become
Ignoring data modeling entirely is professionally negligent
money_quotes: A lack of a data model is still a data model, albeit a crappy one. And users can tell and smell the consequences of this. It smells like a NYC dumpster on a fine summer afternoon.

To say Kimball is irrelevant because of columnar storage and unlimited compute is a dangerously ignorant statement. It is like saying because cars have more horsepower, you don't need to know how to drive a car.
The disturbing trend I've seen is that as we replace data modeling with fancy tools, we're drifting from our ability to think and reason about data.
To completely ignore data modeling is professionally negligent, and I'll argue you're unfit for your job.
Many data practitioners put performance ahead of all else. This is exactly backward.
key_insight: Raw compute power and modern tooling cannot substitute for thoughtful data modeling - prioritizing query performance over conceptual understanding leads to data warehouses that fail to deliver business value.
